changes: ZeroClaw shipped another same-day jump: stable v0.5.9 plus two fresh betas (v0.5.9-beta.579 and v0.5.9-beta.578). The Rust lightweight lane is still one of the fastest-moving branches in the field.
AstrBot advanced again to v4.22.0, which matters because the broader IM-native platform layer is not just active — it is compounding release velocity across consecutive days.

Hermes Agent and OpenClaw both showed fresh repo activity today, but neither changed the top-line strategic read: OpenClaw remains the category anchor, while Hermes remains the strongest learning-loop adjacent benchmark.
PicoClaw pushed a new nightly build, while HiClaw, Poco, IronClaw, NextClaw, and SwarmClaw all continued to show fresh motion or very recent release cadence.
The monitored set itself still did not need to change: Hermes remains the canonical tracked repo at https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent, and the current taxonomy still fits the market.
whyItMatters: The strongest live signal is still sustained release tempo across multiple lanes: lightweight, security-first, orchestration, and adjacent IM platforms are all moving at once.
ZeroClaw keeps reinforcing the case that speed, small footprint, and aggressive shipping cadence are major competitive wedges against the reference implementation.

AstrBot’s back-to-back movement is a reminder that OpenClaw is not just surrounded by clones; it is also pressured by adjacent plugin-heavy IM agent platforms competing for the same operational workflows.
Hermes, IronClaw, NanoClaw, and Poco keep the learning-loop + trust boundary conversation alive, which means the category is still widening on architecture, not only on features.
This is still ecosystem deepening, not taxonomy reset: more momentum, broader proof of demand, same core market map.
